City at the Crossroads
nytimes_articles.abstract:
The key to peace in Bosnia is a small river city called Brcko. 

     This flat little city lies at the narrowest point of the 15-mile-long Serb-held corridor along the Sava River, the border between Bosnia and Croatia.  For the Serbs, the corridor is the link between Serbia and the Serb-held territories in northwestern Bosnia. For the Bosnian Government in Sarajevo, it is a wall that denies Bosnians access to Croatia and the rest of Europe.

Real Estate;In Edgewater, N.J., luxury apartment construction is reviving on the Hudson shoreline.
nytimes_articles.abstract:
By August, work will begin on transforming a patch of the Hudson River shoreline here into a luxury apartment enclave, ending an eight-year dry spell in new residential construction. 

     The $40 million project, the River Club, will also be the first rental to rise amid the condominium and cooperative developments that dot the 3.5-mile-long old industrial waterfront of this Bergen County borough at the base of the Palisade cliffs, south of the George Washington Bridge. Plans call for 266 one- and two-bedroom apartments in seven brick-and-stucco buildings of three stories. The buildings will be clustered into two groups, framing a courtyard and open space.

Caught in Milosevic's Web
nytimes_articles.abstract:
When the United States and its partners pulled together the Bosnian peace accord, they tried to ensnare the region's warmongers in a web of peace. Six months later, it's hard to tell who ensnared whom. 

     The Dayton accord is a tangled web. The President of Serbia, Slobodan Milosevic, who incited and sustained ethnic cleansing and the partition of Bosnia, made the accord possible, but he has not fulfilled his important promises. Last Sunday, he reportedly told Secretary of State Warren Christopher  that he cannot remove the Bosnian Serb leader Radovan Karadzic and Gen. Ratko Mladic, indicted war criminals who foil progress at every turn.

Clean Needles Saved My Life
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Ten years ago, while I was waiting around for my heroin connection, a friend  saved my life. I was at risk for AIDS, she said. To protect myself, I should always use my own works or, if I had to share, I should first clean the needle thoroughly with bleach and water. 

     Thanks to her, I was not infected when I quit two years later. I had survived two diseases: depression, which led me to cocaine and heroin while at Columbia University, and addiction. I was just lucky to have escaped a fatal third.

CLASSICAL MUSIC;Finding Her Way to the Top of Two Worlds
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IT IS ALWAYS TRUE THAT THE popularity generated by a string of flash-in-the-pan successes can fade as quickly as it grew. But a composer who has received commissions from the Los Angeles Philharmonic, the Kronos Quartet and the flutist James Galway, and whose music has been performed by the San Francisco Symphony, the BBC Philharmonic and the American Composers Orchestra, can reasonably be said to have arrived. 

     At 43, Chen Yi is not so much a late bloomer as she is a highly trained musician who has risen to the top in two distinct musical worlds, first in China, then in the United States. By 1978, when she was finally able to enter a  Chinese conservatory after the Cultural Revolution, she was an established composer and the concertmaster of the Beijing Opera Troupe, the national company specializing in the Westernized Socialist Realist style that prevailed at the time.

NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: KEW GARDENS HILLS/FLUSHING;Beneath a Playground, a Burial Ground for 1,000
nytimes_articles.abstract:
For more than three years, the fate of Everett P. Martins Field, a small, run-down park in Flushing, has hung in limbo. In 1993, after a local civic leader presented evidence of a burial ground beneath the playground, a $1.2 million renovation project was delayed pending an archeological evaluation. 

     This spring, the Department of Parks and Recreation commissioned an urban archeologist, Linda Stone, to study the site on 46th Avenue. Her analysis confirmed the research of the civic leader who raised the issue, Mandingo Osceola Tshaka: there may be, Ms. Stone estimates, more than 1,000 bodies beneath the playground.

Exploring the Treatment of Jazz in the Movies
nytimes_articles.abstract:
AT the mention of "Kansas City," Robert Altman's movie that made its debut at the Cannes Film Festival, Krin Gabbard's lively demeanor grows even more animated. 

     Mr. Gabbard, who was just elected chairman of the comparative studies department at the State University at Stony Brook, is the author of "Jammin' at  the Margins," a provocative look at the depiction of jazz in American movies from "The Jazz Singer" (1927) through "Short Cuts" (1993), Mr. Altman's previous film.

COVER STORY;Texas Period Piece Is a Family Affair
nytimes_articles.abstract:
LILY DALE" is about a family in which compromises have been made. The film is a period piece set in Texas in 1910, and evokes the genteel ways of a bygone  era. But beneath the placid surface lie heartaches that can't be mended. The film actress Mary Stuart Masterson stars as Lily Dale in this adaptation of a Horton Foote play, tonight at 10 on Showtime. Her father, Peter Masterson, who directed Horton Foote's "Trip to Bountiful," in which Geraldine Page won an Oscar in 1985, directs, making the movie a family affair. 

      "Acting," says Ms. Masterson, 30, over a recent dinner, "is one of the weirder jobs you can have - playing with your identity and your most private feelings - i n front of strangers." Ms. Masterson has, in movies large and small, a special affinity for roles that explore relationships, especially inside families: sister and brother in "Benny and Joon," husband and wife in "Married to It," a young fiancee and wife in "Gardens of Stone." But performing  for her father in a movie in which she had to convey an unspoken tension between a girl and her stepfather presented its own special challenges.

PRACTICAL TRAVELER;Hotels a Little Like Home
TELEVISION;A Perilous Journey in a Prime-Time Sea
nytimes_articles.abstract:
IT WAS, BY ANY DEFINITION, AN unusual concoction. At its heart were a couple  named Noah and Jesse Beckett and their unlikely menagerie of children and animals. A scene that looked as if it had been plucked from "Lassie," with kids  trailing animals through verdant fields, might dissolve into a moment of "Thirtysomething" angst, with Noah weeping in front of his computer as Wagner played in the background. 

     The series, on Monday evenings at 8, was almost unsettling in its egalitarian construction. The eight children, who ranged in age from 2 to 17, were all adopted. Two were Hispanic, one was black; all had been either abandoned or abused, as had the family's 37 animals. Jesse (Betsy Brantley) was a veterinarian at a wildlife park in Tampa, Fla. Noah (Daniel Hugh Kelly) was a coach turned best-selling author. Nevertheless, the family was in many ways traditional -- they laughed, they fought, they loved. And when the hourlong drama was slipped into ABC's schedule in February, it reinforced values like honesty, loyalty and responsibility.

HELPING;Trying to Patch the Holes in the Safety Net (and, Sometimes, the Roof)
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Mary Cameron is 73 now, and Alzheimer's disease has taken her memory. She has been known to wander around Newark in a fog, looking for offices where she can pay her bills with what little money she has. 

     Routine maintenance has long been a luxury she cannot afford. In the row house where she and her husband built their lives, windows are broken and the roof no longer holds back the rain. Heat costs $400 a month in winter, and ceilings and walls on the top floor are falling in. As Mary takes me through the dark rooms, where only the dog sleeps now, I can feel the wet rugs below my feet and the dried dog droppings.

Persepective;Small-Town Idol Stays True Blue And On Course
A Design Revolutionary at Home
nytimes_articles.abstract:
WILLIAM MORRIS, the Victorian visionary of multiple talents, died a century ago, an occasion that is being commemorated throughout this year by spiritual heirs as diverse as social theorists and interior decorators. Those celebrating  the centennial are gathering at conferences and exhibitions on literary, artistic and political themes -- and making pilgrimages to destinations in England linked to Morris's dream of imbuing everyday life with beauty. 

     Two shrines in particular are likely to attract the pilgrims. One is Kelmscott Manor, an Elizabethan farmstead west of Oxford that Morris leased in summer, described by a contemporary as "the resort of poets and artists, critics and connoisseurs, disciples and aspirants . . . brilliant and memorable." The second is Wightwick Manor, an opulent West Midlands country house that epitomizes the Arts and Crafts Movement, an outgrowth of Morris's reverence for  traditional artisanship.

nytimes_articles.headline: NEIGHBORHOOD REPORT: NEW YORK UP CLOSE;90 Minutes Less in Parking Quest
nytimes_articles.abstract:
One of the defining hardships known to almost every New Yorker with a car, the three-hour alternate-side parking ban, is starting to change, neighborhood by neighborhood. 

     The three-hour ban on parking is being halved to 90 minutes by many community boards. By the end of next year, as many as 20 of the 56 districts where alternate-side rules apply could have shorter parking limits in place (Staten Island is exempt). Five districts have adopted the new rules and two others are  putting them into place. Four boards have recently approved the change, and nine will consider it this year or next.

WHAT'S DOING IN;Portland, Ore.
nytimes_articles.abstract:
Portland is famous for so many things: beer, books, the Oregon rain, coffee -- and what's that other thing? Oh yes, roses. 

     With all the attention paid to Portland's many other attributes, sometimes it's hard to stop and smell the roses. But now that the Portland Rose Festival is upon us, there are ample reminders that this is indeed the City of Roses, so named because its soil, ample rain and mild climate are ideal for rose growing.

WEEKEND GETAWAYS;Remembrance Of Newports Past
nytimes_articles.abstract:
NO matter how you approach Newport, you must cross over water, a fact that delights sailors like us. The aptly named city by the sea occupies the southern  end of Aquidneck Island where Narragansett Bay empties into the ocean. Newport's history has been shaped by the sea that has brought in turn prosperity, decline and, finally, new riches, from the purses of the 3.7 million visitors that flood its shores every year, especially in summer. 

     At the foot of Newport Bridge, the second of two that cross the bay, is one of the town's historic districts, the Point. In this quiet backwater overlooked  by many tourists, we headed for the Elliott Boss House, circa 1820, our B & B for the night. Tom and Loretta Goldrick had been restoring the simple Federal house for 15 years, and their attention to detail was apparent, from the wide, polished floorboards to the authentic working fireplaces.

EARNING IT;Summertime, and Working in a Home Office Isn't Easy
nytimes_articles.abstract:
VERY year about this time, Elaine Carey has a meeting with her children, Alexander, 12, and Sofia, 13. The subject of the talk is Ms. Carey's home office and the rules the children must follow in the summer so they do not disrupt her  work. 

     "My husband and I remind the kids that I am working and am not here as their  summer entertainment counselor," she said.

The View From: Milford;Old Station Is Reborn as an Arts Hub
nytimes_articles.abstract:
THE railroad station in Milford has found new life. The handsomely restored 1881 station (formerly a lamp factory as well as an art gallery) near the green  is the home of the Milford Fine Arts Center. Now an arts hub, the building has been included in the Register of Historic Places by the state. 

     Since the 70's, Milford's cultural groups had been searching banks, churches  and government buildings for display and performance space. After years of bureaucratic frustration, fund-raising events and a campaign, art and craft shows, recitals, workshops and dance performances are finally taking place in the 114-year old building.

Talkers Are Taking Over The Arts, Even Libraries
VOICES: VIEWPOINT;Hearing Alarm Bells In Those Cold Calls
nytimes_articles.abstract:
INVESTORS, brokers, readers: listen up.  There are impostors among us. Would-be brokers, unlicensed and dangerously devoid of knowledge, are often cold-calling prospective clients, opening accounts and, in some cases, executing trades. The National Association of Securities Dealers, the industry watchdog, has been unable to stop these abuses of its own rules, despite some effort. 

     Brokers -- or, more appropriately, "registered representatives" -- are required to pass the N.A.S.D. Series 7 and 63 examinations. The first of these is a difficult, 250-question, six-hour test that many first- and second-time takers fail. It is not uncommon for some people to study for it full time for a  month or so. The best firms offer in-house classroom training to prepare their new hires for the test. But other firms don't. In fact, some firms actually want, and help, their own new hires to fail.
